title: Real Estate Investing 101 — SFR Rentals (for Beginners)
audience: TRUE beginners — assume zero prior knowledge of real estate investing
scriptNotes: v3-final per Matt 2026-05-04: slower speech (no atempo speedup), longer intro for beginners, line-by-line cash flow breakdown, storytelling tone (2nd-person present-tense), no transactional brief language. Targets ~2.0 wps natural pace; ~270 words = ~135s.

segments.headline: Why real estate?
segments.text: Most people don't realize this. For the typical American household, your home is the single biggest piece of your net worth. Not stocks. Not retirement accounts. Real estate. So real estate isn't some advanced strategy. It's the foundation almost everyone is already standing on. Today let me show you how it actually builds wealth.

segments.headline: Picture a rental property
segments.text: Picture a typical rental. A five hundred thousand dollar single-family home. You put twenty-five percent down. That's a hundred and twenty-five thousand. The bank lends you the rest. You rent it for three thousand a month. We're going to follow this property for the next twenty years.

segments.headline: Pillar 1: Cash flow
segments.text: Each month, three thousand dollars in rent comes in. Out goes the mortgage payment, about twenty-five hundred. Property tax, six hundred and twenty-five. Insurance, eighty dollars. And reserves for repairs and vacancies, around two hundred. What's left? About two hundred dollars a month. Not life-changing. But it's income you didn't trade your hours for.

segments.headline: Pillar 2: Appreciation
segments.text: While you're collecting that rent, the house itself is doing something else. It's quietly going up in value. Long-term, U.S. homes have appreciated about three percent a year. On a five hundred thousand dollar home, that's fifteen thousand in year one. By year twenty, you're sitting on roughly four hundred thousand dollars in pure appreciation.

segments.headline: Pillar 3: Loan paydown
segments.text: Here's the part most people miss. Every month, your tenant writes a check. That check pays down YOUR mortgage. Year one, almost four thousand dollars of YOUR loan disappears. Paid by someone else. After thirty years, the mortgage is gone. The property is yours, free and clear.

segments.headline: Pillar 4: Tax benefits
segments.text: Now here's the part the IRS quietly hands you. You can depreciate the building over twenty-seven and a half years. On our example, that's about fourteen thousand five hundred a year you write off. At a typical tax bracket, that's roughly thirty-five hundred dollars a year you keep instead of sending to the government.

segments.headline: Stack all four together
segments.text: Now watch what happens when you stack all four pillars together. Year three, you've built about seventy-six thousand dollars in equity. Year five, one hundred thirty-one thousand. Year ten, two hundred eighty-four thousand. By year twenty, you're looking at six hundred eighty-one thousand dollars.

segments.headline: The honest part
segments.text: Here's the honest part. These are illustrative numbers. Your market and your tax situation will give different results. Vacancies happen. Markets cycle. But the framework — four engines on one property, working at the same time — that's the part that holds. D M me for the spreadsheet that runs your numbers.

intro-hook.claim: For the typical American household, your home is the single biggest piece of your net worth
intro-hook.source: Federal Reserve Survey of Consumer Finances 2022 + Z.1 Financial Accounts of the United States. For households below the top 10% by net worth, primary-residence equity is the largest single component of household wealth. Source URLs: federalreserve.gov/publications/files/scf23.pdf + federalreserve.gov/releases/z1/
